g2u13.gi.adverbs-manner.gf.007.note: Carrier 'The dog can run very ___.' has no disambiguating context, so all three distractors yield fully grammatical, true sentences: 'can run very well', 'can run very slowly', 'can run very badly'. Only the intended-target convention (fast = irregular adverb) separates 'fast' from 'well'; 'The dog can run very well' is unimpeachable English. Grader does exact button match, so a defensible pick is mis-graded.

g2u13.gi.adverbs-manner.gf.002.note: Carrier 'Please close the door ___. The child is in bed.' -> quietly. Distractors 'slowly' and 'nicely' are grammatical, sensible imperatives ('close the door slowly' to avoid noise is defensible given the same context). Context cues quietly but does not exclude them.

g2u13.gi.adverbs-manner.gf.006.note: Carrier 'She did her homework ___ because she wanted to watch TV.' -> quickly. Distractor 'badly' is grammatical and plausibly motivated by the same clause (rushing to watch TV -> did it badly). 'She did her homework badly because she wanted to watch TV' reads as correct English.

g2u13.gi.will-future.gf.001.note: Carrier 'It's cold! I ___ close the window.' -> will (spontaneous decision). Distractor 'can' yields the natural, grammatical offer 'I can close the window.' Same systemic 'can' modal-distractor issue recurs in will gf.002 and (as 'can't') gf.006; each produces a grammatical alternative reading. Grader exact-matches the button, so 'can' is mis-graded as wrong despite being acceptable English.

g2u13.gi.will-future.gf.002.note: Carrier 'Don't worry, I ___ help you with your homework.' -> will. Distractor 'can' yields the natural, grammatical offer 'I can help you with your homework.' Defensible alternative reading; same pattern as gf.001.

g2u13.gi.will-future.gf.006.note: Carrier 'It's a big job. We ___ be at school before nine.' -> won't. Distractor 'can't' yields 'We can't be at school before nine,' which is grammatical and arguably MORE natural for inability given 'It's a big job.' Strong ambiguity between negated-future and negated-ability.
